book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
To Rechared, King of the Visigoths.

ccel.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

source.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

title: To Rechared, King of the Visigoths.

source.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Rechared, &c.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I cannot express in words, most excellent son, how much I am delighted with thy work and thy life.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For on hearing of the power of a new miracle in our days, to wit that the whole nation of the Goths has through thy Excellency been brought over from the error of Arian heresy to the firmness of a right faith, one is disposed to exclaim with the prophet, This is the change wrought by the right hand of the Most High (Ps. lxxvi.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For whose breast, even though stony, would not, on hearing of so great a work, soften in praises of Almighty God and love of thy Excellency?

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As for me, I declare that it delights me often to tell these things that have been done through you to my sons who resort to me and often together with them to admire.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: These things also for the most part stir me up against myself, in that I languish sluggish and unprofitable in listless ease, while kings are labouring in the gathering together of souls for the gains of the heavenly country.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: What then shall I say to the coming Judge in that tremendous assize, if I shall then come thither empty, where thy Excellency shall bring after thee flocks of faithful ones, whom thou hast now drawn to the grace of a true faith by assiduous and continual preaching?

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But this, good man, by the gift of God, affords me great comfort, that the holy work which I have not in myself I love in thee.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, when I rejoice with great exultation for thy doings, the results of thy labour become mine through charity.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: With regard, therefore, to the conversion of the Goths, both for your work and for our exultation, we may well exclaim with the angels, Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace to men of goodwill (Luk. ii.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For we, as I think, owe the more thanks to Almighty God for that, although we have done nothing with you, we are nevertheless partakers in your work by rejoicing with you.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Further, how gladly the blessed Peter, Prince of the Apostles, has accepted the gifts of your Excellency your very life witnesses evidently to all.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For it is written, The vows of the righteous are his delight (Prov. xv.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For indeed in the judgment of Almighty God it is not what is given, but by whom it is given, that is regarded.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For hence it is that it is written, The Lord had respect unto Abel and to his gifts, but unto Cain and to his gifts he had not respect (Gen. iv.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: To wit, being about to say that the Lord had respect to the gifts, he was careful to premise that He had respect unto Abel.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Thus it is plainly shewn that the offerer was not acceptable by reason of the gifts, but the gifts were so by reason of the offerer.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: You shew, therefore, how acceptable your offering is, seeing that, being about to give gold, you have first given gifts of souls by the conversion of the nation subject to you.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: With regard to your telling us that the abbots who were sent to us to bring your offering to the blessed Apostle Peter had been wearied by the violence of the sea and returned to Spain without accomplishing their voyage, your gifts were not kept back, for they reached us afterwards; but the constancy of those who had been sent has been tried, as to whether they knew how with holy desire to overcome dangers in their way, and, though fatigued in body, by no means to be wearied in mind.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For adversity which comes in the way of good purposes is a trial of virtue, not a judgment of reprobation.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For who can be ignorant how prosperous an event it was that the blessed Apostle Paul came to Italy to preach, and yet in coming suffered shipwreck?

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But the ship of the heart stood unharmed among the billows of the sea.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Furthermore, I must tell you that I have been led to praise God the more for your work by what I have learnt from the report of my most beloved son Probinus the presbyter; namely that, your Excellency having issued a certain ordinance against the perfidy of the Jews, those to whom it related attempted to bend the rectitude of your mind by offering a sum of money; which your Excellency scorned, and, seeking to satisfy the judgment of Almighty God, preferred innocence to gold.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: With regard to this what was done by King David recurs to my mind, who, when the longed for water from the cistern of Bethlehem, which was wedged in by the enemy, had been brought him by obedient soldiers, said, God forbid that I should drink the blood of righteous men (1 Chron. xi.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, because he poured it out and would not drink it, it is written, He offered it a libation to the Lord.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If, then, water was scorned by the armed king, and turned into a sacrifice to God, we may estimate what manner of sacrifice to Almighty God has been offered by the king who for His love has scorned to receive, not water, but gold.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore, most excellent son, I will confidently say that thou hast offered as a libation to the Lord the gold which thou wouldest not have in opposition to Him.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: These are great things, and redound to the praise of Almighty God.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But in the midst of all these things we must guard with vigilant attention against the snares of the ancient foe, who, the greater gifts he sees among men, with the more subtle snares seeks to take them away.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For robbers too do not look out for empty travellers to seize them on their road, but such as carry vessels of gold and silver.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For indeed the present life is a road.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And every one must needs be the more on his guard against ambushed spirits in proportion as the gifts are greater which he carries.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: It is the duty, then, of your Excellency, with regard to this so great gift which you have received in the conversion of the nation subject to you, to keep with all your might, first humility of heart, and secondly cleanness of body.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For where it is written, Every one that exalteth himself shall be humbled, and he that humbleth himself shall be exalted (Luke xiv.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: 14), it is assuredly evident that he truly loves what is lofty who does not cut off his soul from the root of humility.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For often the malignant spirit, in order to destroy the good that previously he had not power to oppose, comes into the mind of the worker after accomplishment of his work, and agitates it with silent thoughts of self-praise, so that the deluded mind admires itself for the great things that it has done.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, being exalted in its own sight through hidden tumour, it is deprived of the grace of Him Who bestowed the gift.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For hence it is that it is said through the voice of the prophet to the soul that waxes proud, Having trust in thy beauty thou playedst the harlot because of thy renown (Ezek. xvi.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For indeed a soul’s having trust in its beauty is its presuming within itself on its righteous doings.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And it plays the harlot because of its renown, when in what it has done aright it desires not the praise of its Maker to be spread abroad, but seeks the glory of its own reputation.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence again it is written through the prophet, In that thou art more beautiful, go down (Ezek. xxxii.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For the soul goes down because of being more beautiful when, owing to the comeliness of virtue whereby it ought to have been exalted before God, it falls from His grace through elation.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: What then is to be done in this case but that, when the malignant spirit employs the good things that we have done to exalt the mind, we should ever recall to memory our evil deeds, to the end that we may acknowledge that what we have done sinfully is our own, but that it is of the gift of Almighty God alone when we avoid sins.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Cleanness also of body is to be guarded in our strivings after well-doing, since, according to the voice of the apostolic preacher, The temple of God is holy, which temple ye are (1 Cor. iii.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And again he says, For this is the will of God, even your sanctification (1 Thess. iv.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As to which sanctification, what he means by it he shews by straightway adding, That ye should abstain from fornication, that every one of you should know how to possess his vessel in sanctification and honour, not in the lusts of concupiscence.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: The very government also of your kingdom in relation to your subjects ought to be tempered with moderation, lest power steal upon your mind.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For a kingdom is ruled well when the glory of reigning does not dominate the disposition.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Care also is to be taken that wrath creep not in, lest whatever is lawful to be done be done too hastily.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For wrath, even when it prosecutes the faults of delinquents, ought not to go before the mind as a mistress, but attend as a handmaid behind the back of reason, that it may come to the front when bidden.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, if once it begins to have possession of the mind, it accounts as just what it does cruelly.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For hence it is written, The wrath of man worketh not the righteousness of God (Jam. i.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence again it is said, Let every man be swift to hear, but slow to speak, and slow to wrath (Ib.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: However I doubt not that under the guidance of God you observe all these things.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Still, now that an opportunity of admonition has arisen, I join myself furtively to your good deeds, so that what you do though not admonished you may not do alone, having an admonisher to boot.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now may Almighty God protect you in all your doings by the stretching out of His heavenly arm, and grant you prosperity in the present life, and after a course of many years eternal joys.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have sent you a small key from the most sacred body of the blessed apostle Peter to convey his blessing, containing iron from his chains, that what had bound his neck for martyrdom may loose yours from all sins.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have given also to the bearer of these presents, to be offered to you, a cross in which there is some of the wood of the Lord’s cross, and hairs of the blessed John the Baptist, from which you may ever have the succour of our Saviour through the intercession of His forerunner.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover we have sent to our most reverend brother and fellow-bishop Leander a pallium from the See of the blessed Apostle Peter, which we owe both to ancient custom, and to your character, and to his goodness and gravity.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: A long time ago, when a certain Neapolitan youth came hither, your to me most sweet Excellency had thought fit to charge me to write to the most pious Emperor to the end that he might search in the record office for the treaties that had formerly been concluded with the prince Justinian of pious memory as to the claims of your kingdom, so as to gather from them what he should observe with regard to you.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But there were two things seriously in the way of my doing this.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: One was that the record-office in the time of the aforesaid prince Justinian of pious memory had been so burnt by a fire which had crept in suddenly that hardly any paper of his times remained.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: The other was that, as no one need be told, thou oughtest to look in thy own archives for the documents that are against thee, and produce these instead of my doing so.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Wherefore I exhort your Excellency to arrange matters suitably to your character, and carefully to carry out whatever makes for peace, that the times of your reign may be memorable with great praise through many courses of years.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Furthermore, we have sent you another key from the most sacred body of the blessed apostle Peter, which, being laid up with due honour, may multiply with blessing whatever it may find you enjoying.
